Made with MyST

11. Categorical predictors and interactions

MATH 4210, Chapter 11

Español

In 2008 a US college pulled together a table of its faculty: for each of 397 professors, the table recorded rank, academic discipline, years since the PhD, years of service, sex, and the nine-month academic salary. The table was assembled for a specific reason, to monitor whether men and women were paid differently. That is a question with real stakes, and it is also a question that a single number cannot answer well. The average male salary in this dataset is about $115,090 and the average female salary is about $101,002, a raw gap of roughly $14,088. Is that gap the whole story?

Almost certainly not, because salary depends on more than one thing. It depends heavily on rank: full professors out-earn assistant professors by a wide margin, as Figure 1 shows. It depends on discipline and on years of service. If the mix of ranks, disciplines, and experience differs between the two groups, then a raw average difference blends the effect of sex with the effects of everything else. To say anything careful, we need a model that can hold those other variables fixed. But rank, discipline, and sex are not numbers. They are categories, and so far every predictor in this book has been a measured quantity like lot size or years. This chapter is about putting categories into a regression.

By the end you will be able to turn a categorical predictor into indicator variables and read each coefficient. You will combine a category with a continuous predictor to fit parallel or separate lines, test which fit the data supports, and model curvature with polynomials the right way. And you will see that the one-way analysis of variance you may remember from intro stats is just a regression on indicators. The salary data carries us through the categorical part; the classic cars stopping-distance data carries us through the polynomial part.

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Putting categorical predictors into regression with indicator coding, combining a category with a continuous predictor for parallel or separate lines, and adding interactions and polynomials.

  • Why we are doing it: Salary depends on rank, discipline, and sex, which are categories, not numbers, and a raw male-minus-female gap blends sex with rank and experience, so we need a model that can hold the others fixed.

  • Main objective: Encode a kkk-level factor with k−1k-1k−1 indicators, read each coefficient as a difference of group means or of slopes, and see that one-way analysis of variance is regression on indicators.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Every predictor so far was a measured quantity; from here predictors can be categories, curves, and their combinations, still inside the least-squares framework of 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form and tested with the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test.

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Encode a categorical predictor with reference-cell (indicator) coding, and derive why a factor with kkk levels needs k−1k-1k−1 indicators.

  • Interpret each coefficient of an indicator-coded model as a difference of group means, and explain what the intercept means.

  • Fit and interpret an ANCOVA-style model with one categorical and one continuous predictor as parallel lines.

  • Interpret an interaction coefficient as a difference of slopes, and diagnose the common wrong reading of the main-effect coefficient that comes with it.

  • Test whether two or more groups share a slope using the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test.

  • Fit a polynomial regression with centered predictors, explain why centering helps, and apply the hierarchy principle.

  • Show that one-way analysis of variance is regression on indicator variables, and connect its FFF test to the regression FFF test.

11.1 From categories to numbers: indicator coding¶

Intuition¶

A regression equation multiplies each predictor by a number, so a predictor has to be a number. “Rank” is not: its values are the words Assistant, Associate, and Full. You might be tempted to code them 1, 2, 3 and feed that in. Resist. Coding rank as 1, 2, 3 forces a specific and probably false claim on the data. It says the salary step from Assistant to Associate equals the step from Associate to Full, because both are a move of one unit on your invented scale. The categories have an order, but nothing says the gaps are equal, and a nominal predictor like discipline or sex has no order at all.

The honest way to carry a category into a regression is with indicator variables (Definition 11.1), also called dummy variables: 0-or-1 columns, each answering a yes-or-no question (“is this professor a full professor?”). Indicators let each category have its own level, free of any assumption about spacing. The only subtlety is that you do not use one indicator per category. You use one fewer, and let the left-out category set the baseline. Figure 2 shows the idea for the three ranks: one group becomes the baseline, and the coefficients measure how far each other group sits above it.

Definition 11.1: Indicator (dummy) variable

An indicator variable (or dummy variable) is a column that equals 1 when a case belongs to a specified category and 0 otherwise. It turns a yes-or-no category membership into a number a regression can multiply by a coefficient.

Formula¶

Suppose a categorical predictor (a factor, Definition 11.2) has kkk levels. Pick one level as the reference (or baseline). For each of the other k−1k-1k−1 levels, define an indicator variable that is 1 when a case is in that level and 0 otherwise. For the three ranks, taking Assistant as the reference, define

X1={1if Associate0otherwise,X2={1if Full0otherwise.X_1 = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if Associate} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}, \qquad X_2 = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if Full} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}.X1​={10​if Associateotherwise​,X2​={10​if Fullotherwise​.

  • X1X_1X1​ is the Associate indicator: 1 for associate professors, 0 for everyone else.

  • X2X_2X2​ is the Full indicator: 1 for full professors, 0 for everyone else.

  • There is no indicator for Assistant. An assistant professor has X1=X2=0X_1 = X_2 = 0X1​=X2​=0; the two zeros are the code for the reference level.

Figure 3 shows this coding at work on the first few professors in the data: the single column of rank words becomes two 0-or-1 columns, and the assistant professor’s row is just a pair of zeros.

Definition 11.2: Factor

A factor is a categorical predictor: a predictor whose values are named categories (its levels) rather than measured quantities. A factor with kkk levels enters a regression through k−1k-1k−1 indicator variables, never as a single number.

The model with these two indicators is

Yi=β0+β1Xi1+β2Xi2+εi.Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \beta_2 X_{i2} + \varepsilon_i .Yi​=β0​+β1​Xi1​+β2​Xi2​+εi​.

In words: the mean salary is a baseline β0\beta_0β0​, plus an extra β1\beta_1β1​ if the professor is an associate, plus an extra β2\beta_2β2​ if the professor is full. Read the mean response one level at a time by plugging in the indicators:

E{Y∣Assistant}=β0,E{Y∣Associate}=β0+β1,E{Y∣Full}=β0+β2.E\{Y \mid \text{Assistant}\} = \beta_0, \qquad E\{Y \mid \text{Associate}\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1, \qquad E\{Y \mid \text{Full}\} = \beta_0 + \beta_2 .E{Y∣Assistant}=β0​,E{Y∣Associate}=β0​+β1​,E{Y∣Full}=β0​+β2​.

So β0\beta_0β0​ is the mean of the reference group, β1\beta_1β1​ is the Associate-minus-Assistant difference in means, and β2\beta_2β2​ is the Full-minus-Assistant difference. The coefficients are contrasts against the baseline, nothing more.

This is reference-cell coding (Definition 11.3; R and Python call it treatment coding). It is the default in both languages, and the one this book uses throughout. Chapter 13 will code the predictors of a logistic regression exactly the same way (11.1 From categories to numbers: indicator coding is the pattern it reuses).

Definition 11.3: Reference-cell coding

Reference-cell coding (also called treatment coding) represents a kkk-level factor by choosing one level as the reference and defining k−1k-1k−1 indicators for the other levels, while keeping the intercept. The intercept is then the reference group’s mean, and each coefficient is a group’s mean minus the reference mean.

Derivation (why k−1k-1k−1 indicators, not kkk)¶

Theorem 11.4: A kkk-level factor needs k−1k-1k−1 indicators

Consider a factor with kkk levels. If the design matrix holds the intercept column of ones together with all kkk level indicators X1,…,XkX_1,\dots,X_kX1​,…,Xk​, then

X1+X2+⋯+Xk=1,X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_k = \mathbf{1},X1​+X2​+⋯+Xk​=1,

so the columns are linearly dependent, X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is singular, and the least-squares coefficients are not unique. Dropping one indicator (reference-cell coding) restores full column rank and makes the coefficients identifiable.

The reason is easier to see than to say. Figure 4 lays out the design matrix with the intercept column and all three rank indicators. In every row exactly one indicator is a 1, so adding the three indicator columns together rebuilds the intercept column of ones. One column is then a copy of the others combined, which is the linear dependence that sinks the fit.

Proof. Suppose you stubbornly include all kkk indicators, X1,…,XkX_1, \dots, X_kX1​,…,Xk​, one per level, alongside the intercept column of ones. Every case belongs to exactly one level, so exactly one of its indicators is 1 and the rest are 0. Adding the indicator columns across all levels therefore reconstructs the all-ones column:

X1+X2+⋯+Xk=1.X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_k = \mathbf{1} .X1​+X2​+⋯+Xk​=1.

That is a linear dependence among the columns of the design matrix X\mathbf{X}X. From 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form, the least-squares estimate b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y needs X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X to be invertible, which requires the columns of X\mathbf{X}X to be linearly independent (full column rank). With the intercept plus all kkk indicators, they are not: the coefficients are not uniquely determined, because you could add any constant to β0\beta_0β0​ and subtract it from every level’s effect and get the identical fitted values. Drop one indicator (equivalently, drop the intercept) and the dependence is gone. Reference-cell coding drops the reference level’s indicator, keeps the intercept, and gives a full-rank design with k−1k-1k−1 indicators. ■\blacksquare■

This “one fewer than the number of levels” fact has a name you will meet again: a factor with kkk levels contributes k−1k-1k−1 degrees of freedom to the model.

R¶

R stores categories as factors and builds the indicators for you. Read the data and look at the categorical columns.

salaries <- read.csv("data/salaries.csv")
dim(salaries)
table(salaries$rank)
table(salaries$discipline)
table(salaries$sex)
[1] 397   6

AssocProf  AsstProf      Prof
       64        67       266

  A   B
181 216

Female   Male
    39    358

By default R orders factor levels alphabetically and makes the first one the reference. For rank that would make AssocProf the baseline, which is awkward to read. We reorder so that Assistant is the reference, matching Figure 2.

Example 11.1: Salary by rank with reference-cell coding

Question. Fit salary on rank alone. What does each coefficient mean, and do the coefficients match the group means?

Intuition. With Assistant as the reference, the intercept should be the assistant-professor mean and each coefficient should be a difference of group means.

Formula. β0=E{Y∣Assistant}\beta_0 = E\{Y \mid \text{Assistant}\}β0​=E{Y∣Assistant}, β1=E{Y∣Associate}−E{Y∣Assistant}\beta_1 = E\{Y\mid\text{Associate}\} - E\{Y\mid\text{Assistant}\}β1​=E{Y∣Associate}−E{Y∣Assistant}, β2=E{Y∣Full}−E{Y∣Assistant}\beta_2 = E\{Y\mid\text{Full}\} - E\{Y\mid\text{Assistant}\}β2​=E{Y∣Full}−E{Y∣Assistant}.

Computation.

salaries$rank <- factor(salaries$rank,
                        levels = c("AsstProf", "AssocProf", "Prof"))
fit_rank <- lm(salary ~ rank, data = salaries)
coef(fit_rank)
tapply(salaries$salary, salaries$rank, mean)
  (Intercept) rankAssocProf      rankProf
     80775.99      13100.45      45996.12
 AsstProf AssocProf      Prof
 80775.99  93876.44 126772.11

In Python, statsmodels reads the same file. We make rank an ordered category so Assistant is the reference, then fit with the C() categorical marker.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.formula.api import ols

salaries = pd.read_csv("data/salaries.csv")
salaries["rank"] = pd.Categorical(
    salaries["rank"], categories=["AsstProf", "AssocProf", "Prof"]
)
fit_rank = ols("salary ~ C(rank)", data=salaries).fit()
print(fit_rank.params)
print(salaries.groupby("rank", observed=True)["salary"].mean())
Intercept               80775.985075
C(rank)[T.AssocProf]    13100.452425
C(rank)[T.Prof]         45996.123948
dtype: float64
rank
AsstProf      80775.985075
AssocProf     93876.437500
Prof         126772.109023
Name: salary, dtype: float64

Interpretation. The intercept, $80,776, is exactly the assistant-professor mean. The coefficient on the Associate indicator, $13,100, is exactly the associate mean minus the assistant mean (93,876−80,77693{,}876 - 80{,}77693,876−80,776). The Full coefficient, $45,996, is the full-professor mean minus the assistant mean (126,772−80,776126{,}772 - 80{,}776126,772−80,776). The regression has not invented anything: with a single factor it simply reports each group’s mean, re-expressed as a baseline plus differences.

Key idea

A regression on a single factor invents nothing. It reports each group’s mean, re-expressed as a baseline (the reference mean) plus differences. Every indicator coefficient is a contrast against the reference, so reading the coefficient table is just reading group means.

You can see the indicators themselves in the model’s design matrix. The first column is the intercept (all ones), and the next two are the Associate and Full indicators.

head(model.matrix(fit_rank), 4)
  (Intercept) rankAssocProf rankProf
1           1             0        1
2           1             0        1
3           1             0        0
4           1             0        1
print(fit_rank.model.exog[:4])
[[1. 0. 1.]
 [1. 0. 1.]
 [1. 0. 0.]
 [1. 0. 1.]]

Rows 1, 2, and 4 are full professors (Full indicator 1); row 3 is an assistant professor (both indicators 0). No row ever has both indicators equal to 1, because no professor holds two ranks at once.

A note on other codings

Reference-cell coding is not the only choice. Effects coding (sometimes called sum-to-zero coding) uses −1/0/+1-1/0/+1−1/0/+1 columns so that the intercept becomes the grand mean and each coefficient measures a level’s departure from that grand mean. Cell-means coding drops the intercept and uses all kkk indicators, so each coefficient is a group mean outright. All three give the identical fitted values, residuals, and FFF tests; they differ only in what the individual coefficients mean. This book uses reference-cell coding because “difference from a baseline” is usually the most natural thing to report, but if you ever see an intercept described as a grand mean, someone has switched codings on you.

Try it 11.1

A colleague codes discipline as a single numeric column, A = 0 and B = 1, and fits salary ~ discipline_num. A second colleague codes three ranks as 1, 2, 3 in one numeric column. Which fit is harmless and which one imposes a false assumption? Explain.

Solution

The two-level discipline coding is harmless. A single 0/1 column is the reference-cell indicator for a two-level factor: the intercept is the mean of discipline A and the slope is the B-minus-A difference. With only two levels there is nothing to misspace.

The three-rank coding 1, 2, 3 is the dangerous one. Using a single numeric column forces the model to treat the Assistant-to-Associate step and the Associate-to-Full step as equal (each is “+1 unit,” multiplied by the same slope). The data flatly reject that: the first step is about 13k and the second is about 33k. Ranks need two indicators, not one numeric code, so each step can be its own size.

Durable skill: Recognize when a number is really a label

Zip codes, jersey numbers, Likert responses, product SKUs, and rank codes all look like numbers and arrive in numeric columns, but arithmetic on them is meaningless: zip code 93311 is not “more” than 90210. Before you put any column into a model, ask whether its numbers measure a quantity or just name a category. If they name a category, encode it as a factor and let the software build indicators. This habit prevents a whole class of silent modeling errors, and it transfers to every dataset you will ever touch, in any field.

Coefficients that are really differences of means are easier to trust once you have moved the means yourself and watched the coefficients answer.

11.2 One categorical and one continuous predictor¶

Intuition¶

A category by itself just sorts cases into groups. The interesting models mix a category with a measured predictor. Salary, for instance, depends both on discipline (a category, theoretical A or applied B) and on years of service (a number). We now have two knobs, and there are three increasingly flexible ways to turn them, drawn schematically in Figure 6.

The simplest model ignores the category and fits one line to everybody. The next model, the subject of this section, gives each discipline its own intercept but makes them share a single slope: two parallel lines. Reading it is easy: experience is worth the same number of dollars per year in both disciplines, and one discipline simply sits a fixed amount above the other at every experience level. This parallel-lines model has a traditional name, analysis of covariance or ANCOVA (Definition 11.5), from the days when the continuous predictor was called a covariate and the category was the treatment. The third model, separate slopes, is the next section.

Formula¶

Let XXX be years of service and let DDD be the discipline-B indicator (D=1D = 1D=1 for applied, D=0D = 0D=0 for theoretical). The parallel-lines model is

Yi=β0+β1Xi+β2Di+εi.Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 D_i + \varepsilon_i .Yi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+β2​Di​+εi​.

In words: salary is a baseline, plus a per-year effect of service that both disciplines share, plus a fixed bump for being in discipline B.

  • β1\beta_1β1​ is the slope on years of service, shared by both disciplines.

  • β2\beta_2β2​ is the vertical gap between the two discipline lines, the same at every XXX.

Split it by discipline to see the two lines:

Theoretical (D=0):E{Y}=β0+β1X,Applied (D=1):E{Y}=(β0+β2)+β1X.\text{Theoretical } (D=0): \quad E\{Y\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X, \\ \text{Applied } (D=1): \quad E\{Y\} = (\beta_0 + \beta_2) + \beta_1 X .Theoretical (D=0):E{Y}=β0​+β1​X,Applied (D=1):E{Y}=(β0​+β2​)+β1​X.

In words: both lines have slope β1\beta_1β1​; the applied line is shifted up by β2\beta_2β2​. The two lines never cross, because they are parallel. The reading of β2\beta_2β2​ is a genuine “holding years of service fixed” comparison: at any given experience, applied faculty are paid β2\beta_2β2​ more on average, exactly the kind of adjusted comparison the chapter opened by asking for.

Definition 11.5: Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA)

An analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) model has one categorical and one continuous predictor and no interaction,

Yi=β0+β1Xi+β2Di+εi.Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 D_i + \varepsilon_i .Yi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+β2​Di​+εi​.

It gives each category its own intercept but a single shared slope, so the fitted lines are parallel and β2\beta_2β2​ is the constant vertical gap between them.

R¶

Example 11.2: Parallel lines for salary

Question. Fit salary on years of service and discipline as parallel lines. How much is a year of service worth, and how large is the discipline gap once service is held fixed?

Intuition. One slope for service, one intercept shift for discipline.

Formula. E{Y}=β0+β1(service)+β2DE\{Y\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{service}) + \beta_2 DE{Y}=β0​+β1​(service)+β2​D, with β1\beta_1β1​ the shared slope and β2\beta_2β2​ the discipline gap.

Computation.

fit_par <- lm(salary ~ yrs.service + discipline, data = salaries)
coef(summary(fit_par))
              Estimate Std. Error   t value      Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 91335.8324  3005.3614 30.390965 2.484794e-105
yrs.service   862.7749   109.1542  7.904184  2.732146e-14
disciplineB 13184.0432  2846.8313  4.631129  4.947251e-06
fit_par = ols("salary ~ yrs_service + discipline", data=salaries).fit()
print(fit_par.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, :4].round(4))
                      Coef.   Std.Err.        t  P>|t|
Intercept        91335.8324  3005.3614  30.3910    0.0
discipline[T.B]  13184.0432  2846.8313   4.6311    0.0
yrs_service        862.7749   109.1542   7.9042    0.0

(The Python code renames the yrs.service column to yrs_service after reading the file, because the dot in the original name is not valid inside a statsmodels formula. The R name keeps the dot, which R allows.)

Interpretation. The shared slope is $863 per year of service: in both disciplines, each additional year of service is associated with about $863 more salary on average. The discipline coefficient is $13,184: at any fixed level of service, applied (B) faculty earn about $13,184 more than theoretical (A) faculty on average. Both are far from zero relative to their standard errors. Figure 7 draws the two fitted lines; they run parallel, separated by that constant $13,184 gap.

Try it 11.2

In the parallel model, a theoretical (A) professor and an applied (B) professor both have 10 years of service. Use the fitted coefficients to predict each one’s salary, and confirm their difference is the discipline coefficient.

Solution

Theoretical: Y^=91,335.83+862.77(10)+13,184.04(0)=91,335.83+8,627.7=99,963.5\hat Y = 91{,}335.83 + 862.77(10) + 13{,}184.04(0) = 91{,}335.83 + 8{,}627.7 = 99{,}963.5Y^=91,335.83+862.77(10)+13,184.04(0)=91,335.83+8,627.7=99,963.5. Applied: Y^=91,335.83+862.77(10)+13,184.04(1)=99,963.5+13,184.04=113,147.6\hat Y = 91{,}335.83 + 862.77(10) + 13{,}184.04(1) = 99{,}963.5 + 13{,}184.04 = 113{,}147.6Y^=91,335.83+862.77(10)+13,184.04(1)=99,963.5+13,184.04=113,147.6. The difference is 113,147.6−99,963.5=13,184.0113{,}147.6 - 99{,}963.5 = 13{,}184.0113,147.6−99,963.5=13,184.0, the discipline coefficient exactly. Because the model is parallel, that gap is the same whether you compare at 10 years, 0 years, or 30 years.

11.3 Interactions: letting the slopes differ¶

Intuition¶

The parallel model makes a strong assumption: a year of service is worth the same in both disciplines. Maybe it is not. Maybe applied faculty, whose skills track a hot outside job market, gain more per year than theoretical faculty. If so, the two lines should have different slopes, fanning apart as service grows rather than staying a fixed distance apart. Letting the slopes differ is what an interaction (Definition 11.6) does. An interaction between a category and a continuous predictor says the effect of the continuous predictor depends on the category.

You build it by adding one more column: the product of the continuous predictor and the indicator. That product is zero for the reference group (so its line is unchanged) and equals the continuous predictor for the other group (so that group gets an extra slope term).

Formula¶

With XXX = years of service and DDD = discipline-B indicator, the interaction model is

Yi=β0+β1Xi+β2Di+β3(XiDi)+εi.Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 D_i + \beta_3 (X_i D_i) + \varepsilon_i .Yi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+β2​Di​+β3​(Xi​Di​)+εi​.

In words: take the parallel-lines model and add one product term, which lets discipline B have its own slope instead of sharing the baseline slope.

  • β3\beta_3β3​ is the interaction coefficient, the coefficient on the product XDX DXD.

  • The product term XiDiX_i D_iXi​Di​ is 0 when Di=0D_i = 0Di​=0 and equals XiX_iXi​ when Di=1D_i = 1Di​=1.

Split by discipline again:

Theoretical (D=0):E{Y}=β0+β1X,Applied (D=1):E{Y}=(β0+β2)+(β1+β3)X.\text{Theoretical } (D=0): \quad E\{Y\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X, \\ \text{Applied } (D=1): \quad E\{Y\} = (\beta_0 + \beta_2) + (\beta_1 + \beta_3) X .Theoretical (D=0):E{Y}=β0​+β1​X,Applied (D=1):E{Y}=(β0​+β2​)+(β1​+β3​)X.

In words: the theoretical line has intercept β0\beta_0β0​ and slope β1\beta_1β1​; the applied line has intercept β0+β2\beta_0 + \beta_2β0​+β2​ and slope β1+β3\beta_1 + \beta_3β1​+β3​. So β2\beta_2β2​ is the gap between the two intercepts (the difference at X=0X = 0X=0), and β3\beta_3β3​ is the gap between the two slopes (the extra dollars per year that applied faculty gain over theoretical faculty). If β3=0\beta_3 = 0β3​=0 the slopes are equal and we are back to parallel lines, which is why testing β3=0\beta_3 = 0β3​=0 is the test for “do the slopes differ.”

Definition 11.6: Interaction

An interaction is a product term added to a model so that the effect of one predictor depends on the value of another. For a continuous predictor XXX and an indicator DDD, the interaction term XDX DXD gives each category its own slope: the model

Yi=β0+β1Xi+β2Di+β3XiDi+εiY_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 D_i + \beta_3 X_i D_i + \varepsilon_iYi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+β2​Di​+β3​Xi​Di​+εi​

has slope β1\beta_1β1​ when D=0D = 0D=0 and slope β1+β3\beta_1 + \beta_3β1​+β3​ when D=1D = 1D=1. In a model containing an interaction, a main effect coefficient is the effect only where the interacting variable equals zero.

The wrong reading, first¶

Here is where careful reading matters, because the interaction model sets a trap. Look at the fitted coefficients (Example 11.3 below computes them): the discipline coefficient β2\beta_2β2​ comes out to about $857 with a large standard error and a p-value of 0.86, nowhere near significant. A hurried reader concludes: “discipline does not matter for salary.” That reading is wrong, and the mistake is worth dwelling on.

In the interaction model, β2\beta_2β2​ is not “the effect of discipline.” It is only the gap between the disciplines at X=0X = 0X=0, that is, for a professor with zero years of service. At zero service the two lines nearly meet, so of course that particular gap is small and uncertain. But the lines are not parallel; the applied line climbs faster. Out where the actual faculty live, at 20 or 30 years of service, the disciplines are far apart. Figure 8 makes the point: β2\beta_2β2​ measures the gap at the far-left edge of the plot, which says nothing about the gap in the body of the data.

The correct statement is: the interaction coefficient β3\beta_3β3​ is significant, so the slopes differ, and the discipline gap grows with service. The gap at a specific service level XXX is β2+β3X\beta_2 + \beta_3 Xβ2​+β3​X, not β2\beta_2β2​ alone. Whenever a model contains an interaction, the coefficient on a main effect is a conditional statement about the point where the other predictor is zero, never a blanket “effect.”

R¶

Example 11.3: An interaction between service and discipline

Question. Do the two disciplines gain salary at different rates per year of service?

Intuition. Add the product of service and the discipline indicator, and inspect the interaction coefficient β3\beta_3β3​.

Formula. E{Y}=β0+β1X+β2D+β3XDE\{Y\} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 D + \beta_3 X DE{Y}=β0​+β1​X+β2​D+β3​XD, slopes β1\beta_1β1​ (A) and β1+β3\beta_1 + \beta_3β1​+β3​ (B).

Computation. In R the * in a formula means “both main effects and their interaction,” so yrs.service * discipline expands to yrs.service + discipline + yrs.service:discipline.

fit_int <- lm(salary ~ yrs.service * discipline, data = salaries)
coef(summary(fit_int))
                          Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             98038.0407  3626.8893 27.030889 1.109256e-91
yrs.service               526.8293   150.0725  3.510498 4.991238e-04
disciplineB               857.3892  4750.7379  0.180475 8.568727e-01
yrs.service:disciplineB   695.1651   215.8791  3.220159 1.387754e-03
fit_int = ols("salary ~ yrs_service * discipline", data=salaries).fit()
print(fit_int.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, :4].round(4))
                                  Coef.   Std.Err.        t   P>|t|
Intercept                    98038.0407  3626.8893  27.0309  0.0000
discipline[T.B]                857.3892  4750.7379   0.1805  0.8569
yrs_service                    526.8293   150.0725   3.5105  0.0005
yrs_service:discipline[T.B]    695.1651   215.8791   3.2202  0.0014

Interpretation. The theoretical (A) slope is $527 per year. The interaction coefficient is $695 with a p-value of 0.0014, so the applied (B) slope is 527+695=$1,222527 + 695 = \$1{,}222527+695=$1,222 per year, more than double. Applied salaries climb with service about $695 per year faster than theoretical salaries. The discipline coefficient $857 (p = 0.86) is only the gap at zero years of service, and reading it as “no discipline effect” would be the mistake of the previous section. Figure 9 shows the two lines fanning apart.

Testing whether the slopes really differ¶

Eyeballing a fanned pair of lines is not proof. The interaction added exactly one term to the parallel model, so we can ask whether that one term earns its place with the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test: fit the reduced (parallel) model and the full (interaction) model, and compare their error sums of squares. Here the reduced model is fit_par from Example 11.2 and the full model is fit_int.

anova(fit_par, fit_int)
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: salary ~ yrs.service + discipline
Model 2: salary ~ yrs.service * discipline
  Res.Df        RSS Df  Sum of Sq      F   Pr(>F)
1    394 3.0594e+11
2    393 2.9807e+11  1 7864744229 10.369 0.001388 **
from statsmodels.stats.anova import anova_lm
print(anova_lm(fit_par, fit_int))
   df_resid           ssr  df_diff       ss_diff          F    Pr(>F)
0     394.0  3.059377e+11      0.0           NaN        NaN       NaN
1     393.0  2.980729e+11      1.0  7.864744e+09  10.369424  0.001388

The FFF statistic is 10.37 on 1 and 393 degrees of freedom, with a p-value of 0.0014. That p-value equals the p-value of the interaction coefficient’s ttt test above, and indeed t2=3.222=10.37=Ft^2 = 3.22^2 = 10.37 = Ft2=3.222=10.37=F, the same equivalence between the ttt and FFF tests you saw for a single slope in 3.7 The F test and its equivalence to the t test. Adding the interaction significantly reduces the error sum of squares, so the data support separate slopes over parallel lines.

Try it 11.3

Using the interaction coefficients from Example 11.3, compute the fitted salary gap between an applied and a theoretical professor at 5 years of service and at 35 years of service. Explain in one sentence why the parallel model could not produce these two different gaps.

Solution

The gap at service XXX is b2+b3X=857.39+695.17Xb_2 + b_3 X = 857.39 + 695.17 Xb2​+b3​X=857.39+695.17X. At X=5X = 5X=5: 857.39+695.17(5)=857.39+3,475.85=4,333.2857.39 + 695.17(5) = 857.39 + 3{,}475.85 = 4{,}333.2857.39+695.17(5)=857.39+3,475.85=4,333.2. At X=35X = 35X=35: 857.39+695.17(35)=857.39+24,330.95=25,188.3857.39 + 695.17(35) = 857.39 + 24{,}330.95 = 25{,}188.3857.39+695.17(35)=857.39+24,330.95=25,188.3. The gap grows from about $4,300 to about $25,200. The parallel model forces one constant gap at every service level (its β2\beta_2β2​ alone), so it cannot let the gap widen with experience; only the interaction term, which multiplies the indicator by service, can.

Durable skill: In a model with an interaction, never read a main effect alone

The single most common misreading of a regression table is treating a main-effect coefficient as “the effect of that variable” when the model also contains that variable’s interaction. It is not. It is the effect at the point where the interacting variable equals zero. Train yourself to spot an interaction term in the output first, and, if one is present, to describe effects as “depends on” statements with a specific value plugged in, not as single numbers. This one habit will save you from wrong conclusions in fields from medicine to marketing, wherever “the effect of A depends on B” is the actual finding.

The trap in reading β2\beta_2β2​ is much harder to fall for after you have watched the gap grow under your own finger.

Adjustment and the salary-equity question¶

We opened with a raw male-minus-female gap of about $14,088. Now we can ask what happens to that gap when the model holds rank, discipline, and years of service fixed. This is the statistical heart of an equity analysis: a raw difference can shrink, vanish, or grow once you account for other measured variables, and honest reporting shows the estimate before and after adjustment.

Example 11.4: The sex coefficient, before and after adjustment

Question. How does the estimated male-minus-female salary difference change when rank, discipline, and years of service enter the model?

Intuition. Fit a model with sex alone, then a model that also contains the other predictors, and compare the sex coefficient.

Formula. Raw: E{Y}=β0+βsex (Male)E\{Y\} = \beta_0 + \beta_{\text{sex}}\,(\text{Male})E{Y}=β0​+βsex​(Male). Adjusted: add rank indicators, the discipline indicator, and years of service.

Computation. The raw gap is a two-level factor, so salary ~ sex gives it directly.

tapply(salaries$salary, salaries$sex, mean)
raw <- lm(salary ~ sex, data = salaries)
coef(summary(raw))
  Female     Male
101002.4 115090.4
             Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 101002.41   4809.386 21.001103 2.683482e-66
sexMale      14088.01   5064.579  2.781674 5.667107e-03

Now the adjusted model.

fit_adj <- lm(salary ~ rank + discipline + yrs.service + sex,
              data = salaries)
round(coef(summary(fit_adj)), 2)
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)   68351.67    4482.20   15.25     0.00
rankAssocProf 14560.40    4098.32    3.55     0.00
rankProf      49159.64    3834.49   12.82     0.00
disciplineB   13473.38    2315.50    5.82     0.00
yrs.service     -88.78     111.64   -0.80     0.43
sexMale        4771.25    3878.00    1.23     0.22
raw = ols("salary ~ sex", data=salaries).fit()
print(salaries.groupby("sex")["salary"].mean())
print(raw.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, :4].round(4))
fit_adj = ols(
    "salary ~ C(rank) + discipline + yrs_service + sex", data=salaries
).fit()
print(fit_adj.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, :4].round(2))
sex
Female    101002.410256
Male      115090.418994
Name: salary, dtype: float64
                   Coef.   Std.Err.        t   P>|t|
Intercept    101002.4103  4809.3860  21.0011  0.0000
sex[T.Male]   14088.0087  5064.5792   2.7817  0.0057
                         Coef.  Std.Err.      t  P>|t|
Intercept             68351.67   4482.20  15.25   0.00
C(rank)[T.AssocProf]  14560.40   4098.32   3.55   0.00
C(rank)[T.Prof]       49159.64   3834.49  12.82   0.00
discipline[T.B]       13473.38   2315.50   5.82   0.00
sex[T.Male]            4771.25   3878.00   1.23   0.22
yrs_service             -88.78    111.64  -0.80   0.43

Interpretation. The unadjusted male-minus-female difference is $14,088 (p = 0.006). After adjusting for rank, discipline, and years of service, the estimated difference falls to $4,771 with a standard error of $3,878, so its p-value is 0.22 and it is no longer distinguishable from zero at conventional levels. Figure 11 shows both estimates with their intervals. The adjusted coefficient is an association in observational data, not a measured causal effect: it estimates the average male-minus-female salary difference among faculty who share the same rank, discipline, and years of service. Whether adjusting for rank is even the right thing to do is itself a substantive question. Rank could lie on the causal pathway: if promotion practices differ by sex, then controlling for rank would remove part of the very difference one wants to measure. A regression can produce the adjusted number cleanly; deciding which variables belong in the adjustment set is a question about the world, not about the arithmetic.

Reading an adjusted coefficient honestly

“After adjustment the difference is not significant” is a statement about this model and this sample, not a verdict that pay is equitable. A non-significant coefficient can reflect a genuinely small difference, a small subgroup (only 39 of the 397 faculty are women, so the estimate is imprecise), or an adjustment set that has absorbed part of the effect. Report the estimate, its uncertainty, and the choices behind the adjustment; do not round a p-value up into a conclusion.

11.4 Polynomial regression and centering¶

Intuition¶

Categories are one way a straight line can fail to fit; curvature is another. Stopping distance does not grow in a straight line with speed. Physics says braking distance rises with the square of speed, because kinetic energy does, and the classic cars data (50 cars from the 1920s) shows exactly the upward bend, drawn in Figure 12. A straight line undershoots at the high speeds where the curve turns up. These are the same cars data whose residual funnel drove the square-root transformation in 10.3 Box-Cox: letting the data choose the power; there we reshaped the response, and here we leave it alone and let the mean function bend instead.

We can bend a regression line by adding the square of the predictor as a second term. A polynomial regression (Definition 11.7) keeps the model linear in its coefficients (so all the least-squares machinery still applies) while making the fitted curve a parabola, or a cubic, or higher. The one practical wrinkle is that a predictor and its square are usually strongly related to each other, which makes the raw coefficients unstable and hard to read. The fix is centering (Definition 11.8): subtract the mean of the predictor before squaring.

Formula¶

The second-order (quadratic) polynomial model in a predictor XXX is

Yi=β0+β1Xi+β2Xi2+εi.Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 X_i^2 + \varepsilon_i .Yi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+β2​Xi2​+εi​.

In words: the straight-line model with one extra column, the square of the predictor, which lets the fitted curve bend instead of staying straight.

  • β2\beta_2β2​ controls the curvature: positive bends the parabola upward, negative bends it down.

  • The model is still a linear model, because it is linear in β0,β1,β2\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2β0​,β1​,β2​; the predictor X2X^2X2 is just another column.

Definition 11.7: Polynomial regression

A polynomial regression adds powers of a predictor (X2,X3,…X^2, X^3, \dotsX2,X3,…) as extra columns so the fitted curve can bend, while the model stays linear in its coefficients. The second-order (quadratic) model is

Yi=β0+β1Xi+β2Xi2+εi,Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 X_i^2 + \varepsilon_i ,Yi​=β0​+β1​Xi​+β2​Xi2​+εi​,

with β2\beta_2β2​ controlling the curvature.

The centered version replaces XXX by x=X−Xˉx = X - \bar Xx=X−Xˉ:

Yi=β0∗+β1∗xi+β2∗xi2+εi,xi=Xi−Xˉ.Y_i = \beta_0^{\ast} + \beta_1^{\ast} x_i + \beta_2^{\ast} x_i^2 + \varepsilon_i, \qquad x_i = X_i - \bar X .Yi​=β0∗​+β1∗​xi​+β2∗​xi2​+εi​,xi​=Xi​−Xˉ.

In words: measure the predictor as a deviation from its average before squaring. Centering does not change the shape of the fitted curve or any fitted value. It only shifts what the linear and intercept terms mean, and it sharply reduces the correlation between the first-order and second-order columns. After centering, β0∗\beta_0^{\ast}β0∗​ is the fitted mean at the average speed and β1∗\beta_1^{\ast}β1∗​ is the slope of the curve at the average speed, both readable numbers.

Definition 11.8: Centering

Centering a predictor means replacing XXX by the deviation x=X−Xˉx = X - \bar Xx=X−Xˉ before forming its powers. Centering leaves every fitted value unchanged, but it sharply reduces the correlation between the linear and squared columns, which makes the coefficients stable and interpretable.

Why centering helps¶

The raw predictor speed runs from 4 to 25, so speed and speed^2 both increase together across that whole range; their correlation in the cars data is 0.98. Two columns that move almost in lockstep carry nearly the same information, and the regression struggles to divide credit between them: the individual coefficients get large standard errors and can even flip sign. Subtracting the mean recenters the predictor on zero, so its square becomes small in the middle and large at both ends, a U-shape that no longer tracks the linear term. Figure 13 shows the correlation collapsing from 0.98 to about -0.10 after centering.

R¶

Example 11.5: Quadratic stopping distance, raw and centered

Question. Fit a quadratic for stopping distance on speed, first raw and then centered. What changes, and what stays the same?

Intuition. The two fits describe the identical curve; only the meaning of the linear and intercept coefficients, and their standard errors, should change.

Formula. Raw: Y^=b0+b1X+b2X2\hat Y = b_0 + b_1 X + b_2 X^2Y^=b0​+b1​X+b2​X2. Centered: Y^=b0∗+b1∗x+b2∗x2\hat Y = b_0^{\ast} + b_1^{\ast} x + b_2^{\ast} x^2Y^=b0∗​+b1∗​x+b2∗​x2 with x=X−Xˉx = X - \bar Xx=X−Xˉ.

Computation. In a formula, I(speed^2) (R) and I(speed**2) (Python) mean “the literal square of speed,” protecting the exponent from the formula’s own syntax.

cars <- read.csv("data/cars.csv")
fit_lin <- lm(dist ~ speed, data = cars)
fit_quad <- lm(dist ~ speed + I(speed^2), data = cars)
coef(summary(fit_quad))
cor(cars$speed, cars$speed^2)
             Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 2.4701378 14.81716473 0.1667079 0.8683151
speed       0.9132876  2.03422044 0.4489620 0.6555224
I(speed^2)  0.0999593  0.06596821 1.5152647 0.1364024
[1] 0.9794765
cars = pd.read_csv("data/cars.csv")
fit_lin = ols("dist ~ speed", data=cars).fit()
fit_quad = ols("dist ~ speed + I(speed**2)", data=cars).fit()
print(fit_quad.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, :4].round(5))
print(np.corrcoef(cars["speed"], cars["speed"] ** 2)[0, 1])
                 Coef.  Std.Err.        t    P>|t|
Intercept      2.47014  14.81716  0.16671  0.86832
speed          0.91329   2.03422  0.44896  0.65552
I(speed ** 2)  0.09996   0.06597  1.51526  0.13640
0.9794764867466514

Now center speed and refit.

cars$speed_c <- cars$speed - mean(cars$speed)
fit_quad_c <- lm(dist ~ speed_c + I(speed_c^2), data = cars)
coef(summary(fit_quad_c))
cor(cars$speed_c, cars$speed_c^2)
               Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)  40.2411151 2.80596206 14.341290 8.718708e-19
speed_c       3.9920341 0.41189824  9.691797 8.709632e-13
I(speed_c^2)  0.0999593 0.06596821  1.515265 1.364024e-01
[1] -0.09553281
cars["speed_c"] = cars["speed"] - cars["speed"].mean()
fit_quad_c = ols("dist ~ speed_c + I(speed_c**2)", data=cars).fit()
print(fit_quad_c.summary2().tables[1].iloc[:, :4].round(5))
print(np.corrcoef(cars["speed_c"], cars["speed_c"] ** 2)[0, 1])
                    Coef.  Std.Err.         t   P>|t|
Intercept        40.24112   2.80596  14.34129  0.0000
speed_c           3.99203   0.41190   9.69180  0.0000
I(speed_c ** 2)   0.09996   0.06597   1.51526  0.1364
-0.09553281107427546

Interpretation. The quadratic coefficient is 0.09996 in both fits, identical, because centering does not change the curvature. What changes is the linear term. In the raw fit the speed coefficient is 0.913 with a huge standard error (t = 0.45), an artifact of its 0.98 correlation with speed^2. In the centered fit the speed_c coefficient is 3.99 with t = 9.69, now clearly the slope of the curve at the mean speed of 15.4 mph. The centered intercept 40.24 is the fitted stopping distance at that mean speed. Centering did not improve the fit; it made the coefficients interpretable and stable.

Key idea

Centering is a change of coordinates, not a change of model. The fitted curve, its predictions, and R2R^2R2 are identical before and after. Only the intercept and linear coefficient change meaning: they become the height and slope of the curve at the average predictor value instead of at zero.

Claiming that centering changes the coefficients but not the curve is one thing; sliding the centering point yourself and seeing the curve hold still is another.

The hierarchy principle and choosing the degree¶

Two rules keep polynomial models sane.

First, the hierarchy principle (Definition 11.9): if a model includes a higher-order term, keep all the lower-order terms below it, even if they look non-significant. A model with X2X^2X2 but no XXX forces the parabola’s vertex to sit at X=0X = 0X=0. That is an arbitrary constraint the data never asked for, and it is not even preserved if you shift the predictor’s origin. Keep XXX whenever you keep X2X^2X2. The same logic says keep both main effects whenever you keep their interaction, which is why the interaction models in section 11.3 always carried both yrs.service and discipline.

Definition 11.9: Hierarchy principle

The hierarchy principle says that if a model includes a higher-order term, it should keep all lower-order terms beneath it: keep XXX whenever X2X^2X2 is present, and keep both main effects whenever their interaction is present.

Second, do not chase degree. Each extra power bends the curve more and will always fit the sample a little better, but high-degree polynomials wiggle wildly between and beyond the data points. Figure 15 fits degrees 1, 2, and 5 to the cars data: the quadratic tracks the gentle bend, while the degree-5 curve starts chasing individual points and swings at the ends, the classic face of overfitting. Test whether the added curvature is real rather than reading individual high-order coefficients.

We test the quadratic the same way we tested the interaction, with the general linear test: is the reduced (linear) model beaten by the full (quadratic) model?

anova(fit_lin, fit_quad)
predict(fit_quad, newdata = data.frame(speed = 20))
predict(fit_quad_c, newdata = data.frame(speed_c = 20 - mean(cars$speed)))
Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: dist ~ speed
Model 2: dist ~ speed + I(speed^2)
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq     F Pr(>F)
1     48 11354
2     47 10825  1    528.81 2.296 0.1364
       1
60.71961
       1
60.71961
print(anova_lm(fit_lin, fit_quad))
print(fit_quad.predict(pd.DataFrame({"speed": [20]})))
xc = 20 - cars["speed"].mean()
print(fit_quad_c.predict(pd.DataFrame({"speed_c": [xc]})))
   df_resid           ssr  df_diff     ss_diff         F    Pr(>F)
0      48.0  11353.521051      0.0         NaN       NaN       NaN
1      47.0  10824.715908      1.0  528.805143  2.296027  0.136402
0    60.719611
dtype: float64
0    60.719611
dtype: float64

Two things to read here. The FFF for the quadratic term is 2.30 with a p-value of 0.14: for these 50 cars the upward bend is suggestive but not decisive, and a straight line is defensible. The honest report is that the curvature is in the expected direction but not established at conventional significance, exactly the kind of judgment call this course asks you to make and state rather than hide. Second, both the raw and centered models predict the identical stopping distance of 60.72 feet at 20 mph, confirming that centering is a change of parametrization, not a change of model.

Try it 11.4

A student fits dist ~ I(speed^2), dropping the linear term because its p-value was large in the raw quadratic fit. Name the principle this violates, and give one concrete reason the resulting model is a poor choice.

Solution

It violates the hierarchy principle: a model with X2X^2X2 should keep XXX. Concretely, dropping the linear term forces the parabola to have its vertex (its flat point) at speed=0\text{speed} = 0speed=0, a car sitting still, which is an arbitrary and physically meaningless constraint the data never supported. The large p-value on speed in the raw fit was an artifact of its 0.98 correlation with speed^2, not evidence that the linear term is unnecessary; centering (or the joint FFF test) shows the curve genuinely needs both terms to describe its slope away from the vertex.

Overfitting is easier to believe when you make it happen yourself, one degree at a time.

A brief look at piecewise regression¶

Polynomials bend a single smooth curve across the whole range. Sometimes a relationship instead changes slope at a particular point: a dose that helps up to a threshold and hurts after, a tax rate that kicks in at an income level. For these you can fit piecewise linear regression (Definition 11.10), two or more straight segments joined at chosen points called knots. The trick is one extra column: for a knot at ccc, add the “hinge” predictor (X−c)+(X - c)_+(X−c)+​, which equals X−cX - cX−c when X>cX > cX>c and 0 otherwise. Its coefficient is the change in slope at the knot, and because the hinge is continuous the two segments meet without a jump. Figure 17 shows a two-segment fit with a knot at X=5X = 5X=5. This is the simplest member of the spline family; the course does not pursue splines further, but the idea, add hinge columns to let the slope change at knots, is a natural extension of everything in this chapter.

Definition 11.10: Piecewise linear regression

A piecewise linear regression joins two or more straight segments at chosen predictor values called knots. A knot at ccc enters through the hinge column (X−c)+=max⁡(X−c, 0)(X - c)_+ = \max(X - c,\ 0)(X−c)+​=max(X−c, 0), whose coefficient is the change in slope at the knot; continuity across the knot is automatic. It is the simplest member of the spline family.

11.5 Analysis of variance as regression¶

Intuition¶

If you took intro statistics you likely met one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA, Definition 11.11), the FFF test for whether several group means are equal, and you may have met it as a separate technique with its own formulas. It is not separate. One-way ANOVA is exactly the regression of the response on the group factor, coded with indicators, which is the model of section 11.1. Seeing this collapses two things you learned as one and lets every tool of regression, diagnostics, intervals, the general linear test, work on group-comparison problems.

The key fact is that when the only predictor is a factor, the fitted value for every case is just its own group’s sample mean. So the regression “curve” is a set of flat levels, one per group, as Figure 18 shows for the three ranks. Nothing else could minimize the squared error within each group, because the mean is the least-squares constant.

Formula¶

The one-way ANOVA model is usually written

Yij=μ+τi+εij,Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij},Yij​=μ+τi​+εij​,

for observation jjj in group iii, where μ\muμ is a baseline and τi\tau_iτi​ is group iii’s effect. With reference-cell coding this is precisely Y=β0+β1X1+⋯+βk−1Xk−1+εY = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_{k-1} X_{k-1} + \varepsilonY=β0​+β1​X1​+⋯+βk−1​Xk−1​+ε from section 11.1: μ\muμ is the reference group’s mean β0\beta_0β0​, and each τi\tau_iτi​ is a coefficient β\betaβ, the difference of a group’s mean from the reference. The ANOVA null hypothesis that all group means are equal,

H0:τ1=τ2=⋯=τk=0,H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_k = 0,H0​:τ1​=τ2​=⋯=τk​=0,

is the regression hypothesis that all the indicator coefficients are zero, H0:β1=⋯=βk−1=0H_0: \beta_1 = \cdots = \beta_{k-1} = 0H0​:β1​=⋯=βk−1​=0: the factor explains nothing. That is tested by the model’s overall FFF statistic.

Definition 11.11: One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA)

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) is the FFF test of whether several group means are equal. Its model Yij=μ+τi+εijY_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}Yij​=μ+τi​+εij​ is exactly the regression of the response on the group factor coded with indicators, and its null hypothesis H0:τ1=⋯=τk=0H_0:\tau_1 = \cdots = \tau_k = 0H0​:τ1​=⋯=τk​=0 is the regression hypothesis H0:β1=⋯=βk−1=0H_0:\beta_1 = \cdots = \beta_{k-1} = 0H0​:β1​=⋯=βk−1​=0.

Theorem 11.12: One-way ANOVA is regression on indicators

Let a single kkk-level factor be the only predictor, coded with reference-cell indicators. Then the least-squares fitted value for every case is its own group’s sample mean; the regression sum of squares equals the ANOVA between-groups sum of squares; the error sum of squares equals the within-groups sum of squares; and the model’s overall

F=MSRMSEF = \frac{\mathrm{MSR}}{\mathrm{MSE}}F=MSEMSR​

equals the one-way ANOVA FFF statistic on k−1k-1k−1 and n−kn-kn−k degrees of freedom.

R¶

Example 11.6: The rank ANOVA is the rank regression

Question. Show that the one-way ANOVA FFF for salary across ranks is the same FFF the salary- on-rank regression already reported.

Intuition. The anova() table of the regression and the overall FFF of the regression summary should both equal the classic one-way ANOVA FFF.

Formula. F=MSR/MSEF = \mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}F=MSR/MSE with k−1=2k-1 = 2k−1=2 and n−k=394n-k = 394n−k=394 degrees of freedom.

Computation. We reuse fit_rank from Example 11.1.

anova(fit_rank)
summary(fit_rank)$fstatistic
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: salary
           Df     Sum Sq    Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)
rank        2 1.4323e+11 7.1616e+10  128.22 < 2.2e-16 ***
Residuals 394 2.2007e+11 5.5855e+08
   value    numdf    dendf
128.2174   2.0000 394.0000
print(anova_lm(fit_rank))
print(fit_rank.fvalue, fit_rank.df_model, fit_rank.df_resid)
             df        sum_sq       mean_sq           F        PR(>F)
C(rank)     2.0  1.432318e+11  7.161588e+10  128.217394  1.293048e-43
Residual  394.0  2.200689e+11  5.585504e+08         NaN           NaN
128.21739383203618 2.0 394.0

Interpretation. The ANOVA table’s FFF value, 128.22 on 2 and 394 degrees of freedom, is identical to the regression summary’s overall FFF statistic, to every digit. They are the same test computed two ways. The “between-groups sum of squares” of ANOVA is the regression’s SSR, and the “within-groups sum of squares” is the regression’s SSE. There is one machine here, not two.

This bridge pays off in both directions. Everything you know about ANOVA (that a significant FFF says the means differ somewhere, not which pair) transfers to reading a factor’s overall test in a regression. And everything regression offers (residual diagnostics, confidence intervals for specific contrasts, adding a continuous covariate to get the ANCOVA of section 11.2) is now available for problems you might have thought needed a separate ANOVA toolkit. Two levels of the factor make it the two-sample ttt test; more levels make it one-way ANOVA; add a covariate and it is ANCOVA. All one model.

Key idea

The two-sample ttt test, one-way ANOVA, and ANCOVA are not three techniques but one linear model seen at different settings: a two-level factor gives the two-sample test, a kkk-level factor gives ANOVA, and adding a continuous covariate gives ANCOVA. Learn the regression and you get all three.

Try it 11.5

The rank regression has SSR =1.4323×1011= 1.4323 \times 10^{11}=1.4323×1011 on 2 degrees of freedom and SSE =2.2007×1011= 2.2007 \times 10^{11}=2.2007×1011 on 394 degrees of freedom. Compute the FFF statistic by hand and confirm it matches 128.22.

Solution

MSR=SSR/2=1.4323×1011/2=7.1616×1010\mathrm{MSR} = \mathrm{SSR}/2 = 1.4323\times 10^{11}/2 = 7.1616\times 10^{10}MSR=SSR/2=1.4323×1011/2=7.1616×1010. MSE=SSE/394=2.2007×1011/394=5.5855×108\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/394 = 2.2007\times 10^{11}/394 = 5.5855\times 10^{8}MSE=SSE/394=2.2007×1011/394=5.5855×108. F=MSR/MSE=7.1616×1010/5.5855×108=128.2F = \mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE} = 7.1616\times 10^{10} / 5.5855\times 10^{8} = 128.2F=MSR/MSE=7.1616×1010/5.5855×108=128.2. This matches the printed F=128.22F = 128.22F=128.22 (small rounding aside), confirming the ANOVA FFF and the regression FFF are one number.

11.6 Chapter summary¶

You can now bring categories and curves into a regression. You encode a kkk-level factor with k−1k-1k−1 reference-cell indicators, and you can say why one indicator must be dropped: including all kkk plus the intercept makes the design rank-deficient (Theorem 11.4). You read each indicator coefficient as a difference of group means and the intercept as the reference-group mean. You combine a category with a continuous predictor three ways: a common line, parallel lines (the ANCOVA model), and separate slopes (an interaction), and you test between the parallel and separate models with the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test. You know the trap that a main-effect coefficient in an interaction model is only the effect at the other predictor’s zero. You fit a polynomial, center it so its coefficients are stable and interpretable, apply the hierarchy principle, and resist over-fitting the degree. And you see one-way ANOVA as nothing but regression on indicators, with the same FFF test (Theorem 11.12).

Key results at a glance.

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Reference-cell model (Def 11.3)Y=β0+∑j=1k−1βjXj+εY = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\beta_j X_j + \varepsilonY=β0​+∑j=1k−1​βj​Xj​+εone kkk-level factor, one level chosen as reference
Indicator coefficientβj=E{Y∣level j}−E{Y∣ref}\beta_j = E\{Y\mid\text{level }j\} - E\{Y\mid\text{ref}\}βj​=E{Y∣level j}−E{Y∣ref}reference-cell coding
k−1k-1k−1 indicators (Theorem 11.4)X1+⋯+Xk=1X_1+\cdots+X_k=\mathbf{1}X1​+⋯+Xk​=1 forces rank deficiency; drop oneintercept kept in the model
Parallel lines / ANCOVA (Def 11.5)Y=β0+β1X+β2D+εY = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 D + \varepsilonY=β0​+β1​X+β2​D+εone continuous, one categorical, equal slopes
Separate slopes / interaction (Def 11.6)Y=β0+β1X+β2D+β3XD+εY = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 D + \beta_3 X D + \varepsilonY=β0​+β1​X+β2​D+β3​XD+εslopes allowed to differ by group
Between-group gap (interaction)gap(X)=β2+β3X\text{gap}(X) = \beta_2 + \beta_3 Xgap(X)=β2​+β3​Xequals β2\beta_2β2​ only at X=0X = 0X=0
Test of equal slopesgeneral linear test, full vs reduced (t2=Ft^2 = Ft2=F)nested parallel vs interaction models
Quadratic model (Def 11.7)Y=β0+β1X+β2X2+εY = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 X^2 + \varepsilonY=β0​+β1​X+β2​X2+εcurvature; still linear in coefficients
Centering (Def 11.8)x=X−Xˉx = X - \bar Xx=X−Xˉ; curvature β2\beta_2β2​ unchangedto cut linear-square correlation, stabilize coefficients
Hierarchy principle (Def 11.9)keep XXX with X2X^2X2; keep main effects with interactionany higher-order term present
One-way ANOVA = regression (Theorem 11.12)F=MSR/MSEF = \mathrm{MSR}/\mathrm{MSE}F=MSR/MSE equals the ANOVA FFFa single factor is the only predictor

Key terms. indicator (dummy) variable, factor, level, reference-cell coding, effects coding, cell-means coding, analysis of covariance (ANCOVA), parallel lines, interaction, main effect, polynomial regression, centering, hierarchy principle, knot, piecewise linear regression, spline, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA).

You should now be able to.

  • Encode a categorical predictor with reference-cell coding, and explain why a kkk-level factor needs k−1k-1k−1 indicators.

  • Interpret each indicator coefficient as a difference of group means and the intercept as the reference-group mean.

  • Fit and interpret an ANCOVA (parallel-lines) model with one categorical and one continuous predictor.

  • Interpret an interaction coefficient as a difference of slopes, and avoid the wrong reading of the main-effect coefficient that comes with it.

  • Test whether two or more groups share a slope using the general linear test.

  • Fit a polynomial regression with a centered predictor, explain why centering helps, and apply the hierarchy principle.

  • Show that one-way ANOVA is regression on indicator variables, and connect its FFF test to the regression FFF test.

Where this fits. In the workflow spine of The modeling workflow, this chapter is mostly FIT: it widens the kinds of predictors a linear model can hold, from measured quantities to categories, curves, and their combinations, without leaving the least-squares framework of 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form. It leans on the general linear test of 8.4 The general linear test to decide between nested models, which is the CHECK-inside-FIT loop the course uses whenever a model gains a term. It also serves USE: interpreting an interaction, or an adjusted coefficient, is the communication step where most real mistakes happen, which is why the chapter spent so long on wrong readings. Looking ahead, 11.1 From categories to numbers: indicator coding is the exact machinery Chapter 13 uses to put factors into a logistic regression, and the centering and hierarchy habits carry into Chapter 12, where correlated predictors (including a predictor and its own square) drive the study of multicollinearity.

11.7 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. Which category should I make the reference? Any of them; the fit is identical, only the coefficients’ meaning changes. Pick the reference that makes the comparisons you care about easiest to read: a control group, the largest group, or a natural baseline like “assistant professor.” If a coefficient’s sign feels backwards, you may just want to relevel so the other category is the reference.

Q2. Do I ever include an indicator for every level? Only if you drop the intercept. The intercept column plus all kkk indicators are linearly dependent, so software will either drop one automatically or (with the intercept removed) switch to cell-means coding, where each coefficient is a group mean. Never include the intercept and all kkk indicators; the model is not identifiable.

Q3. My interaction is significant but one main effect is not. Do I drop the main effect? No. The hierarchy principle says keep both main effects whenever you keep their interaction. A non-significant main effect in an interaction model usually just means the groups are similar at the point where the other predictor is zero, which is rarely an interesting place and often outside the data. Dropping it distorts the model.

Q4. Why not just fit separate regressions to each group instead of one interaction model? Fitting each group separately gives the same fitted lines as the full interaction model, so for prediction it is fine. But the single interaction model lets you test whether the slopes differ (one coefficient, one p-value) and pools the groups to estimate a common error variance, which separate fits cannot do. When the question is “are these groups different,” one model with an interaction is the cleaner tool.

Q5. Does centering change my predictions or my R2R^2R2? No. Centering is a change of parametrization: it shifts the meaning of the intercept and linear coefficient but leaves every fitted value, residual, R2R^2R2, and FFF statistic untouched. Example 11.5 shows the raw and centered quadratics predicting the identical 60.72 feet at 20 mph. Center for interpretation and numerical stability, not to change the fit.

Q6. How high a polynomial degree should I go? Rarely past two or three, and only with a test to back it up. Each degree fits the sample better but generalizes worse, wiggling between points (Figure 15). Use the general linear test to check whether the added term reduces error significantly, prefer the lowest degree that captures the visible shape, and never extrapolate a high-degree polynomial beyond the data, where it can shoot off to absurd values.

Q7. The adjusted sex coefficient is not significant. Does that prove pay is fair? No. It says that in this sample and this model, once rank, discipline, and service are held fixed, the remaining male-minus-female difference is not distinguishable from zero, with a wide interval driven partly by there being only 39 women. It does not establish equity, and it depends on the choice of adjustment variables; if rank itself is affected by sex, adjusting for it can hide part of the difference. Report the numbers and the assumptions, and let readers judge.

11.8 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use salaries.csv for categorical problems (with Assistant as the rank reference) and cars.csv for polynomial problems. Recall the fitted values used in the chapter: rank means 80,776 / 93,876 / 126,772; parallel model slope 862.77 and discipline gap 13,184; interaction slopes 526.83 (A) and 1,221.99 (B) with interaction 695.17; centered quadratic b2=0.0999b_2 = 0.0999b2​=0.0999 at mean speed 15.4 mph. Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) Explain why coding the three ranks as a single numeric column 1, 2, 3 imposes an assumption the data reject, and say what that assumption is in one sentence.

  2. (A) In the model salary ~ rank with Assistant as reference, state in words what the intercept and each of the two coefficients mean.

  3. (A) A factor has 5 levels. How many indicator variables does reference-cell coding use, and how many degrees of freedom does the factor contribute? Explain.

  4. (A) Describe the difference between the parallel-lines model and the separate-slopes model in terms of what the two fitted lines look like.

  5. (A) In an interaction model salary ~ yrs.service * discipline, a classmate says “the discipline coefficient is not significant, so discipline does not affect salary.” Correct this statement.

  6. (A) Explain the hierarchy principle in one or two sentences, and give an example of a model that violates it.

  7. (A) Why does centering a predictor before squaring leave every fitted value unchanged while changing the linear coefficient’s value and meaning?

  8. (A) State the connection between one-way ANOVA and regression, and say what the ANOVA between-groups and within-groups sums of squares correspond to in the regression.

  9. (B) Prove that including an intercept plus all kkk indicators of a kkk-level factor makes the design matrix rank-deficient (Theorem 11.4), by exhibiting the linear dependence among its columns. Explain why this makes the least-squares coefficients non-unique.

  10. (B) In reference-cell coding, prove that the intercept equals the reference group’s sample mean and each coefficient equals a difference of group sample means, for a one-factor model. Use the fact that the least-squares fitted value in each group is that group’s sample mean.

  11. (B) For the parallel-lines model Y=β0+β1X+β2D+εY = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 D + \varepsilonY=β0​+β1​X+β2​D+ε, write the two fitted lines for D=0D = 0D=0 and D=1D = 1D=1, and prove their vertical distance is β2\beta_2β2​ at every XXX.

  12. (B) For the interaction model Y=β0+β1X+β2D+β3XD+εY = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 D + \beta_3 XD + \varepsilonY=β0​+β1​X+β2​D+β3​XD+ε, derive the fitted gap between the two groups as a function of XXX, and show it equals β2\beta_2β2​ only at X=0X = 0X=0.

  13. (B) Show algebraically that the ttt test of the interaction coefficient (t=3.22t = 3.22t=3.22) and the general linear test of parallel-versus-separate slopes (F=10.37F = 10.37F=10.37) satisfy t2=Ft^2 = Ft2=F, and explain why this must hold for a single added term (cite 3.7 The F test and its equivalence to the t test).

  14. (B) Let x=X−Xˉx = X - \bar Xx=X−Xˉ. Starting from Y=β0+β1X+β2X2Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 X^2Y=β0​+β1​X+β2​X2, substitute X=x+XˉX = x + \bar XX=x+Xˉ and collect terms to express β0∗,β1∗,β2∗\beta_0^{\ast}, \beta_1^{\ast}, \beta_2^{\ast}β0∗​,β1∗​,β2∗​ of the centered model in terms of β0,β1,β2\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2β0​,β1​,β2​ and Xˉ\bar XXˉ. Confirm β2∗=β2\beta_2^{\ast} = \beta_2β2∗​=β2​ (the curvature is unchanged).

  15. (B) Using the result of problem 14, explain precisely why the raw and centered quadratics give identical fitted values and identical residuals, so R2R^2R2 is unchanged.

  16. (B) Prove that in a one-factor regression the least-squares fitted value for each group is the group’s sample mean, by minimizing ∑(Yij−mi)2\sum (Y_{ij} - m_i)^2∑(Yij​−mi​)2 over the group constants mim_imi​.

  17. (B) A model with a three-level factor has coefficients b0,b1,b2b_0, b_1, b_2b0​,b1​,b2​ under reference-cell coding. Derive what the intercept would become under cell-means coding (all three indicators, no intercept), and confirm the fitted values are unchanged.

  18. (B) In the ANCOVA model, show that the estimated slope β1\beta_1β1​ is a pooled within-group slope, and explain in words why pooling assumes the true slopes are equal across groups (the assumption the interaction test checks).

  19. (C) Fit salary ~ discipline and confirm from the coefficients that the intercept is the mean salary in discipline A and the slope is the B-minus-A difference. Report both means.

  20. (C) Fit salary ~ rank with Full professor (Prof) as the reference instead of Assistant. Report the new intercept and coefficients, and verify the fitted group means are identical to the chapter’s.

  21. (C) Fit the parallel model salary ~ yrs.service + sex and the interaction model salary ~ yrs.service * sex. Report the interaction coefficient and its p-value, run the general linear test between the two models, and state whether the slopes differ for men and women.

  22. (C) Reproduce Example 11.4’s adjustment: fit salary ~ sex and salary ~ rank + discipline + yrs.service + sex, report the sex coefficient in each, and write two sentences interpreting the change, being careful not to claim a causal effect.

  23. (C) Fit the raw quadratic dist ~ speed + I(speed^2) and the centered quadratic on cars. Report the correlation between the linear and squared columns in each case, and the standard error of the linear coefficient in each case, and explain the difference.

  24. (C) Fit polynomials of degree 1, 2, and 3 to the cars data. Use the general linear test to decide whether degree 2 improves on degree 1 and whether degree 3 improves on degree 2. State which degree you would report and why.

  25. (C) Using the interaction model salary ~ yrs.service * discipline, predict the salary of a theoretical (A) and an applied (B) professor at 0, 15, and 30 years of service. Tabulate the six predictions and the A-versus-B gap at each service level, and comment on how the gap changes.

  26. (C) Fit dist ~ speed on cars and add the hinge predictor (speed−15)+(\text{speed} - 15)_+(speed−15)+​ to fit a two-segment piecewise model with a knot at 15 mph. Report the two segment slopes (before and after the knot) and say whether the slope increases after the knot.

  27. (C) Verify the ANOVA-regression bridge (Theorem 11.12) numerically: fit salary ~ rank, extract the overall FFF from the model summary and the FFF from anova() (R) or anova_lm (Python), and confirm they are identical. Report both.

  28. (C) Make a residuals-versus-fitted plot for the linear dist ~ speed fit on cars. Describe any pattern you see (curvature, widening spread) and say which remedy from this chapter or an earlier one each pattern would suggest.

11.9 Exam practice¶

These five questions match the style of the course exams: each one asks you to explain in full sentences, not just to compute. Read any output given, say which numbers you used, and write your answer the way you would on the exam. Then open the model answer and compare. A correct number with no reasoning earns little credit here, so practice saying why.

EP 11.1 (interpret output in context, then evaluate a claim). A colleague fits salary on years since the PhD, sex, and their interaction, using female as the reference level for sex.

fit <- lm(salary ~ yrs.since.phd * sex, data = salaries)
round(coef(summary(fit)), 4)
                        Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)           73840.7607  8696.7132  8.4907   0.0000
yrs.since.phd          1644.8825   454.6163  3.6182   0.0003
sexMale               20209.6135  9179.2131  2.2017   0.0283
yrs.since.phd:sexMale  -727.9822   468.0468 -1.5554   0.1207

The colleague reads the table and concludes: “The sexMale coefficient is about $20,200 and it is significant, so men are paid about $20,000 more than women.” Evaluate this claim. In your answer, say what each of the four coefficients means in context, and state what the sex gap actually is at a realistic level of experience.

Model answer

The claim misreads the main-effect coefficient, the classic interaction trap. Because the model contains the product yrs.since.phd:sex, the sexMale coefficient is not the overall male-minus- female gap. It is the gap only where the interacting variable is zero, that is, for a professor with zero years since the PhD. So the honest reading of each coefficient is: the intercept $73,841 is the mean salary of a brand-new female PhD; the yrs.since.phd slope $1,645 per year is how fast female salaries rise with experience; the sexMale coefficient $20,210 is the male-minus-female gap at zero years since the PhD; and the interaction −$728-\$728−$728 is how much less per year the male salary rises, so the male slope is 1,645−728=$9171{,}645 - 728 = \$9171,645−728=$917 per year.

Because the male slope is smaller, the gap does not stay at $20,210, it shrinks with experience. The gap at XXX years since the PhD is 20,209.61−727.98 X20{,}209.61 - 727.98\,X20,209.61−727.98X. At a realistic mid-career value of, say, 20 years, the estimated gap is 20,209.61−727.98(20)≈$5,65020{,}209.61 - 727.98(20) \approx \$5{,}65020,209.61−727.98(20)≈$5,650, far below the $20,000 the colleague quoted, and at about 28 years it closes entirely. On top of that, the interaction is not significant (p=0.12p = 0.12p=0.12), so the data do not firmly establish that the slopes differ at all; the safest statement is that men start ahead but the estimated gap narrows with seniority. A weak answer reports $20,210 as “the effect of sex” and never notices that the coefficient is conditional on zero years of experience.

EP 11.2 (explain why). In Example 11.5 the raw quadratic dist ~ speed + I(speed^2) gives the speed coefficient a standard error of about 2.03 (so t=0.45t = 0.45t=0.45), while the centered quadratic gives the speed_c coefficient a standard error of about 0.41 (so t=9.69t = 9.69t=9.69). The curvature coefficient, the fitted curve, and R2R^2R2 are identical in the two fits. Explain why centering shrinks the standard error of the linear coefficient so dramatically, and explain why this is not evidence that the centered model “fits better.”

Model answer

The two fits are the same parabola written in two coordinate systems, so they have identical fitted values, residuals, SSE, and R2R^2R2; centering changes the parametrization, not the model. What changes is the correlation between the two predictor columns. On the raw scale speed runs from 4 to 25, so speed and speed^2 both climb together and correlate about 0.98. When two columns are nearly collinear the least-squares fit cannot tell how much credit each one deserves, because many different coefficient pairs give almost the same fitted values; this near-singularity inflates the diagonal of (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1, and since s{bj}=s(X′X)jj−1s\{b_j\} = s\sqrt{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}_{jj}}s{bj​}=s(X′X)jj−1​​ the standard error of the linear coefficient blows up. Subtracting the mean before squaring makes the linear column and the squared column nearly uncorrelated (their correlation falls to about -0.10), so each coefficient is pinned down on its own and its standard error collapses.

This is a gain in interpretability and numerical stability, not in fit. The centered speed_c coefficient is now clearly readable as the slope of the curve at the mean speed, with a small standard error, but the model predicts exactly the same stopping distances as before. A weak answer claims centering “improves the fit” or raises R2R^2R2; it does neither, and saying so misses that collinearity hurts the standard errors while leaving the fitted curve untouched.

EP 11.3 (what would change if). Example 11.1 fit salary ~ rank with Assistant as the reference level and reported an intercept of $80,776, a rankAssocProf coefficient of $13,100, and a rankProf coefficient of $45,996. A colleague refits the identical model but sets Full professor (Prof) as the reference instead. Describe what would change in the coefficient table and what would stay exactly the same, and explain why. Give the numerical values the new table would show.

Model answer

Changing the reference level only relabels the comparisons; it does not change the model or its fit. The fitted group means, the residuals, R2R^2R2, and the overall FFF statistic are all identical, because the three fitted salaries are still each group’s own sample mean (Assistant $80,776, Associate $93,876, Full $126,772) no matter which level is chosen as the baseline. What changes is what the intercept and the two coefficients measure. With Full as the reference, the intercept becomes the full-professor mean, and each coefficient becomes a group’s mean minus the full-professor mean, so both coefficients turn negative. The refit produces

               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)   126772.11    1449.07   87.48        0
rankAssocProf -32895.67    3290.47  -10.00        0
rankAsstProf  -45996.12    3230.54  -14.24        0

The intercept $126,772 is the full-professor mean; rankAssocProf =−$32,896= -\$32,896=−$32,896 says associates earn that much less than full professors, and rankAsstProf =−$45,996= -\$45,996=−$45,996 says assistants earn that much less. That last number is exactly the negative of the old rankProf coefficient ($45,996), because the Full-minus-Assistant gap and the Assistant-minus-Full gap are the same distance with opposite sign. A weak answer says the fit itself changes or that one reference is more correct than another; the choice of reference is only a choice of which comparison is easiest to read.

EP 11.4 (interpret output in context, then evaluate a claim). For the cars data a student fits the linear model and the quadratic model and compares them.

fit_lin  <- lm(dist ~ speed, data = cars)
fit_quad <- lm(dist ~ speed + I(speed^2), data = cars)
round(coef(summary(fit_quad)), 4)
anova(fit_lin, fit_quad)
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)   2.4701    14.8172  0.1667   0.8683
speed         0.9133     2.0342  0.4490   0.6555
I(speed^2)    0.1000     0.0660  1.5153   0.1364

Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: dist ~ speed
Model 2: dist ~ speed + I(speed^2)
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq     F Pr(>F)
1     48 11354
2     47 10825  1    528.81 2.296 0.1364

The student concludes: “The squared-speed coefficient is positive, which matches the physics that braking distance grows faster than linearly, so we should report the quadratic model.” Evaluate this conclusion, using the numbers in the output.

Model answer

The student is right about the direction and wrong to treat it as settled. The squared-speed coefficient is +0.100, and a positive curvature does match the physics that stopping distance rises with the square of speed. But a coefficient having the expected sign is not the same as the curvature being established. The general linear test comparing the quadratic to the straight line gives F=2.30F = 2.30F=2.30 on 1 and 47 degrees of freedom with p=0.1364p = 0.1364p=0.1364, well above 0.05, so the drop in error sum of squares (from 11,354 to 10,825) is within what sampling noise could produce. For these 50 cars the upward bend is suggestive but not significant, and a straight line is defensible.

The honest report is exactly that: the curvature is in the physically expected direction but not established at conventional significance, so either model is reasonable and the simpler straight line is a fair default.
